title: The national grid — getting electricity from power station to your home
examTip: High voltage → low current → less power lost as heat in cables (P_lost = I²R). This is WHY the grid uses high voltage — not just 'to save energy' — you need to explain the physics.
introTips: The national grid uses very high voltages — up to 400,000 V — to cut energy waste in cables.
Transformers only work with AC (alternating current) — this is why mains electricity is AC.
The transformer equation: V₁/V₂ = n₁/n₂ — voltage ratio equals turns ratio.
intro: Every time you plug something in, you're using electricity that may have been generated hundreds of miles away — at a wind farm off the coast of Scotland, a nuclear power station in Somerset, or a gas-fired plant in Yorkshire. Getting that electricity to your home without wasting most of it along the way is a fascinating engineering challenge. The solution — using very high voltages for long-distance transmission, then stepping the voltage back down before it reaches your kettle — is one of the cleverest applications of physics in everyday life.

keyPoints.heading: How the national grid works
keyPoints.content: The national grid is the network of cables and transformers that distributes electricity across the UK, from power stations to homes, schools, and businesses. Electricity is generated at power stations at around 25,000 V (25 kV). A step-up transformer then increases this to up to 400,000 V (400 kV) for long-distance transmission through high-voltage overhead cables. At the other end, a series of step-down transformers reduce the voltage in stages — from 400 kV down to 33 kV for heavy industry, 11 kV for lighter industry, and finally to 230 V for homes. Why use such high voltages? From P = IV: if power (P) is constant and voltage (V) is very high, current (I) must be very low. And from P_lost = I²R: lower current means dramatically less power wasted as heat in the resistance of the cables. Doubling the voltage halves the current, and since power loss ∝ I², this cuts cable losses by four times.
writeBullets: National grid — the network of cables and transformers distributing electricity across the UK
Generated at ~25 kV → stepped up to ~400 kV for long-distance transmission
Stepped down to 230 V for homes via a series of step-down transformers
High voltage → low current (P = IV, constant P): I = P/V — higher V means lower I
Low current → less power wasted as heat in cables: P_lost = I²R (power loss ∝ I²)

keyTerms.def: The network of cables and transformers that distributes electricity from power stations to consumers across the UK
Power (W) = current (A) × voltage (V); at constant power, higher V means lower I
Power wasted as heat in cables = current squared × resistance; lower current dramatically reduces heat loss

keyPoints.memTip: High V → low I → low I²R loss. The grid uses high voltage because it squares the current reduction in cable losses.
keyPoints.tutor_note: The I²R equation is the key to explaining WHY high voltage — students must go beyond 'less energy is wasted' and show the physics of why lower current reduces heating losses.

keyPoints.heading: Transformers — how they work
keyPoints.content: Transformers only work with alternating current (AC) — the changing magnetic field from an AC current in the primary coil induces a voltage in the secondary coil. (DC produces a constant magnetic field that does not induce a voltage.) A step-up transformer has more turns on the secondary than the primary — it increases voltage and decreases current. A step-down transformer has fewer turns on the secondary — it decreases voltage and increases current. The transformer equation: V₁/V₂ = n₁/n₂ (primary voltage ÷ secondary voltage = primary turns ÷ secondary turns). For an ideal (100% efficient) transformer: V₁I₁ = V₂I₂ (power in = power out). Worked example: a transformer has 200 turns on the primary and 2000 on the secondary, with an input voltage of 230 V. Output voltage = 230 × (2000 ÷ 200) = 2300 V (step-up, ratio 1:10).
writeBullets: Transformers only work with AC — changing current creates changing magnetic field, which induces a voltage
Step-up transformer — more turns on secondary; increases voltage, decreases current
Step-down transformer — fewer turns on secondary; decreases voltage, increases current
Transformer equation: V₁/V₂ = n₁/n₂ (voltage ratio = turns ratio)
Ideal transformer: V₁I₁ = V₂I₂ (power in = power out, assuming 100% efficiency)

keyTerms.def: A transformer with more turns on the secondary coil than the primary — increases voltage, decreases current
A transformer with fewer turns on the secondary than the primary — decreases voltage, increases current
The transformer equation: ratio of voltages equals ratio of turns on each coil
For an ideal transformer, power input = power output (100% efficient)

keyPoints.memTip: Step UP = more turns on secondary → V goes up, I goes down. Step DOWN = fewer turns → V goes down, I goes up.
keyPoints.tutor_note: A common exam question is to calculate missing values using V₁/V₂ = n₁/n₂, then use V₁I₁ = V₂I₂ to find the secondary current. Practise both steps together.

keyPoints.heading: Generating electricity
keyPoints.content: Most conventional power stations work by: burning fuel (coal, gas, oil) or using nuclear fission (uranium) to release heat → heat water to produce steam → steam drives a turbine → turbine drives a generator → generator produces electricity by electromagnetic induction (spinning a coil inside a magnetic field). Renewable alternatives: wind turbines (wind pushes blades → turbine → generator — no steam needed), hydroelectric (flowing water drives turbines — very reliable), solar photovoltaic (light directly produces electricity from semiconductor cells — no turbine at all). Note: nuclear power still uses the steam-turbine-generator chain, just like fossil fuels — but produces no CO₂ during generation. The key distinction is between PV solar (no turbine) and everything else (turbine required).
writeBullets: Conventional chain: fuel/fission → heat → steam → turbine → generator → electricity
Electromagnetic induction — electricity generated by spinning a coil in a magnetic field
Wind turbine — wind turns blades directly; drives generator (no steam)
Hydroelectric — water flow drives turbine; drives generator (no steam)
Solar PV — light converted directly to electricity; no turbine, no steam, no generator
Nuclear — same steam-turbine-generator chain as fossil fuels; no CO₂ but radioactive waste

keyTerms.def: The process of generating a voltage (and current) by moving a conductor through a magnetic field — how generators work
A device with blades that spin when hit by steam, water, or wind — drives a generator to produce electricity
Technology that directly converts light energy to electrical energy using semiconductor cells — no turbine needed

keyPoints.memTip: MOST energy sources: heat → steam → turbine → generator. Solar PV is the odd one out — straight to electricity.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often forget that nuclear power still uses turbines and steam — it's not fundamentally different from coal, just a different heat source.

keyPoints.heading: Energy sources and the environment
keyPoints.content: The UK uses a mix of energy sources to balance reliability, cost, and environmental impact. Non-renewables: Coal (highest CO₂ per kWh, cheapest to burn, being phased out), natural gas (lower CO₂ than coal, flexible — can be turned on quickly to meet peak demand), nuclear (no CO₂ during generation, reliable baseload, but radioactive waste and high construction costs). Renewables: Solar PV (no emissions, fast installation, cheap at scale, but intermittent — only works when sunny), wind (no emissions, cheapest new electricity source in the UK by 2020s, but intermittent), hydroelectric (reliable, controllable, no emissions, but requires rivers/dams and affects ecosystems), tidal (extremely predictable — tides never stop, but expensive to build), biomass (burns plant material — potentially carbon-neutral if crops regrow, but debated). UK energy mix (approximate 2023): ~40% renewables (mainly offshore wind), ~40% gas, ~15% nuclear, remainder other. The grid needs baseload power (constant, reliable — nuclear), peak load response (flexible — gas), and an expanding share of renewables with energy storage.
writeBullets: Non-renewable — fossil fuels (coal, gas, oil) + nuclear; finite supply; coal highest CO₂
Coal — high CO₂, cheapest to burn, being phased out in the UK
Natural gas — lower CO₂ than coal, flexible peak-load response
Nuclear — no CO₂ in generation, reliable baseload; produces radioactive waste
Solar/wind — zero emissions, now cheapest new electricity, but intermittent (weather-dependent)
Hydroelectric — reliable, controllable, no emissions; needs rivers and dams (ecosystem impact)
Tidal — predictable and reliable; expensive to build and maintain
Biomass — burns plant material; potentially carbon-neutral; debated sustainability

keyTerms.def: Energy source that is naturally replenished and will not run out — wind, solar, hydro, tidal, biomass
Energy source that will run out — fossil fuels (coal, gas, oil) and nuclear (uranium)
Not always available — solar only works when sunny, wind only when it blows
The constant minimum level of electricity demand — met by reliable sources like nuclear and hydroelectric

keyPoints.memTip: Renewables are FREE to run but INTERMITTENT. Non-renewables are RELIABLE but produce CO₂ (or waste). The grid needs both.
keyPoints.tutor_note: 6-mark questions often ask for an evaluation of energy sources — students must give both advantages AND disadvantages, and consider reliability, cost, and environmental impact.

tables.title: Energy sources — comparison for the national grid
rows: High CO₂; air pollution; being phased out
Lower CO₂ than coal; quick to start up
Still emits CO₂; gas prices volatile
No CO₂ in generation; long lifespan
Radioactive waste; high build cost; slow to start
Cheapest new electricity (UK); no fuel cost
Only works when wind blows; visual impact
No moving parts; low maintenance; scalable
Can store energy (pumped hydro); no fuel
Needs suitable geography; dam affects ecosystems
Tides are entirely predictable; long lifespan
Very expensive to build; limited suitable sites
Can use existing coal power stations
Land use; CO₂ neutrality is debated

1.question: A transformer has 500 turns on the primary coil and 5000 turns on the secondary. The input voltage is 230 V. What is the output voltage?
1.correctFeedback: Correct! V₂ = V₁ × (n₂/n₁) = 230 × (5000/500) = 230 × 10 = 2300 V. This is a step-up transformer (ratio 1:10).
1.wrongFeedback: Use V₁/V₂ = n₁/n₂. Rearrange: V₂ = V₁ × (n₂/n₁) = 230 × (5000 ÷ 500) = 230 × 10 = 2300 V. More turns on secondary → higher voltage (step-up).

0.question: Why does the national grid transmit electricity at very high voltages (up to 400,000 V)?
options: High voltage means lower current, which greatly reduces the power wasted as heat in the cables (P_lost = I²R)
High voltage makes the electricity travel faster through the cables
High voltage prevents the cables from overheating due to solar radiation
High voltage is safer for the engineers who maintain the cables
0.correctFeedback: Exactly right! At constant power, higher voltage means lower current (from P = IV). Lower current dramatically reduces cable heating losses because P_lost = I²R — loss is proportional to current squared.
0.wrongFeedback: The answer is about minimising energy waste in the cables. From P = IV: higher V → lower I. From P_lost = I²R: lower I → much less power wasted as heat. That's why the grid uses high voltages — not for speed, and not for safety.

3.question: Which energy source can generate electricity without using a turbine?
3.correctFeedback: Correct! Solar PV directly converts light to electricity using semiconductor cells — no turbine, no steam, no generator. All the others (including nuclear and wind) drive a generator via a turbine.
3.wrongFeedback: Solar PV is the only common electricity source that converts light directly to electricity without a turbine. Nuclear, wind, and hydroelectric all use turbines connected to generators.

commonMistakes: Saying the grid uses high voltage 'to save energy' without explaining the mechanism — you must link it to P = IV (lower current) and P_lost = I²R (lower heating losses).
Thinking transformers work with DC — they only work with AC, because DC produces a constant (not changing) magnetic field, which does not induce a voltage in the secondary coil.
Confusing step-up and step-down transformers — step-up has MORE turns on the secondary (voltage goes UP); step-down has FEWER turns on secondary (voltage goes DOWN).
Forgetting that nuclear power stations still use turbines and steam — the difference from fossil fuels is the heat source (fission, not burning), not the generation method.
Saying solar PV is 'renewable' without noting it is intermittent — you should acknowledge both the advantage (no emissions, free fuel) and the disadvantage (weather-dependent).
examTips: For 'why high voltage in the grid' questions: state (1) P = IV so high V means low I; (2) P_lost = I²R so low I means much less power wasted. Give both equations for full marks.
Transformer equation: V₁/V₂ = n₁/n₂. Check your answer: does the voltage go up if secondary has more turns? If not, you've divided the wrong way.
For 6-mark energy source evaluation questions: give advantages AND disadvantages for each source, and include reliability, environmental impact, and cost.
Remember: transformers need AC because the electromagnetic induction only works with a changing current. This is a common exam question.
revisionCardBullets: National grid: generates at ~25 kV → steps up to ~400 kV → transmits → steps down to 230 V for homes
High voltage → low current (P = IV); low current → less heat loss in cables (P_lost = I²R)
Step-up transformer: more secondary turns → voltage up, current down
Step-down transformer: fewer secondary turns → voltage down, current up
Transformer equation: V₁/V₂ = n₁/n₂ (voltage ratio = turns ratio)
Ideal transformer: V₁I₁ = V₂I₂ (power in = power out)
Transformers only work with AC — DC gives constant magnetic field, no induction
Conventional generation: fuel/fission → heat → steam → turbine → generator
Solar PV exception: light → electricity directly; no turbine, no steam
Nuclear: same steam-turbine chain as fossil fuels; no CO₂ but radioactive waste
Renewables (wind, solar, hydro, tidal) produce no CO₂ but can be intermittent
